coredata.dc:title: K2SiF6:Mn4+ significantly improves water resistance and luminescence performance by coating green emission graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Lin
Wang, Tianman
Deng, Daishu
Zhong, Xue
Yu, Yan
Qiang, Jiawei
Qin, Dongni
Liao, Sen
Huang, Yingheng
coredata.dc:description: In order to enhance the luminescent thermal stability and water resistance of K2SiF6:Mn4+, we proposed a simple hydrothermal method to synthesize K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDS (6 mg/mol) (GE-GQDs: green emission graphene quantum dots) red emitting phosphor with outstanding water resistance. The emission intensity of the sample coated with the GE-GQDs still maintains 87.74% of the initial one after immersion for 360 min, while the uncoated K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+ sharply decreases to 11.26%. Another impressive phenomenon is that due to the negative thermal quenching (NTQ) effect, the luminescent thermal stability of the coated sample is also significantly improved, and the integrated photoluminescence (PL) intensity at 180 °C is 238% of that at 25 °C. In the meantime, the integrated PL intensity of the coated samples is 2.55 times that of K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+ under room temperature. Based on experimental results, it is suggested that after coating with GE-GQDs, a surface protective layer is formed on the surface of the sample, which improves the water resistance, room temperature emission intensity, and luminescent thermal stability of the coated sample. Finally, the improvement mechanism of the above multiple enhancements on luminescent properties is discussed and proposed. The above multiple enhancement effects make the sample have broad application for blue light-based warm and high-power WLEDs (white light-emitting diodes).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green emission graphene quantum dots
Water resistance
Negative thermal quenching
Red emitting phosphors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of Mn4+ and GE-GQDs concentrations on luminescent properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of samples (i-ii), (i) K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+, (ii) K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDs.
SEM image and the corresponding EDS spectra of samples (i-ii), (i) K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+, (ii) K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDs (6 mg/mol): (a) and (b) (i), (c) and (d)(ii).
TEM images of sample K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+ (a), K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDs (6 mg/mol) (b).

PL spectra of (a) K2SiF6:x1Mn4+ and (b) K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@ GE-GQDs (y
mg/mol).

Luminescent properties of samples (i-ii), (i) K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+, (ii) K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDs (6 mg/mol) at room temperature: (a) PLE spectra, (b) PL spectra, (c) CIE chromaticity diagrams, (d) PL spectrum of K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDs (6 mg/mol) from 470 nm to 680 nm, (e) Fluorescence decay curves, (f) Diagram of energy level transition induced by GE-GQDs.
Water resistance test of samples (i-ii), (i) K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+, (ii) K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDs (6 mg/mol) at different times: (a) Photos under natural light, (b) Integrated emission intensity.

Thermal performances of samples (i)-(ii), (i) K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+, (ii) K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDs (6 mg/mol): (a), (b) PL spectra, (c) Relative PL intensity curves, (d) Integrated intensity curves of Stokes and anti-Stokes peaks, (e–f) Temperature-dependent fluorescence decay curves, (g) Chromaticity shift, (h) CIE color coordinates, (i) TG curve of (ii).

Performances of prototype WLEDs prepared by coating mixture of samples (i)-(ii) and YAG:Ce3+ based on InGaN chip: (a) Electroluminescence spectra at 10 mA driven current, (b) CIE chromaticity diagram at 10 mA, (c) CCT at different driven current, (d) Ra at different driven current.

head.ce:title: K2SiF6:Mn4+ significantly improves water resistance and luminescence performance by coating green emission graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to enhance the luminescent thermal stability and water resistance of K2SiF6:Mn4+, we proposed a simple hydrothermal method to synthesize K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDS (6 mg/mol) (GE-GQDs: green emission graphene quantum dots) red emitting phosphor with outstanding water resistance. The emission intensity of the sample coated with the GE-GQDs still maintains 87.74% of the initial one after immersion for 360 min, while the uncoated K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+ sharply decreases to 11.26%. Another impressive phenomenon is that due to the negative thermal quenching (NTQ) effect, the luminescent thermal stability of the coated sample is also significantly improved, and the integrated photoluminescence (PL) intensity at 180 °C is 238% of that at 25 °C. In the meantime, the integrated PL intensity of the coated samples is 2.55 times that of K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+ under room temperature. Based on experimental results, it is suggested that after coating with GE-GQDs, a surface protective layer is formed on the surface of the sample, which improves the water resistance, room temperature emission intensity, and luminescent thermal stability of the coated sample. Finally, the improvement mechanism of the above multiple enhancements on luminescent properties is discussed and proposed. The above multiple enhancement effects make the sample have broad application for blue light-based warm and high-power WLEDs (white light-emitting diodes).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a solid-state light source, white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) are becoming a new generation of lighting sources own to their excellent luminescence efficiency, extra-long lifespan, energy-saving, and environment friendly. They have been extensively used in background lighting, liquid crystal display screens, domestic lighting, and other fields [1–4]. The most common WLEDs are encapsulated by coating the yellow emitting phosphor (YAG:Ce3+) on InGaN blue chip. However, since there is no red emission in their luminescent spectra, the WLEDs are facing the problem of low color rendering index (CRI) and an excessive high correlated color temperature (CCT), which both are critical requirements in practical application [5–9]. The most direct and commonly used way is adding a spectral matched red emitting phosphor to supplement the red-light component [10–16].

Commercial red emission phosphors are mostly Eu2+ doped nitrides [10–13] with higher quantum yields and outstanding thermal stability. However, in most cases, the broad emission band of the Eu2+ doped nitrides is beyond 650 nm and overlaps with the excitation band of the yellow emitting phosphors. Severe reabsorption reduces the luminescent efficiency of WLEDs dramatically. Also, its main emission band is beyond the range of human eye sensitivity [17–19]. Recently the research hotspots have gradually shifted to narrowband emission nitride red phosphors such as Sr [LiAl3N4]:Eu2+ [20,21], its half-peak width of the spectrum is narrower by about 50 nm. Although the reabsorption effect is reduced, the problem of harsh synthetic conditions and high production costs still exist.

Therefore, developing red emitting phosphors with a simple preparation process, high color purity, and low cost is crucial. Mn4+ activated fluoride phosphors have drawn more attention since they were firstly reported by Paulusz et al. [22]. The phosphors have unique properties of narrowband red emission and broad-band blue excitation. Their strongest absorption peak centered at 450 nm matches the emission peak of the InGaN chip, while the emission of 630 nm is completely located within the sensitivity range of the human eye [23,24]. Adachi's team prepared a range of Mn4+ doped fluoride phosphors A2XF6:Mn4+ in 2008 [25]. However, it is found that the Mn4+ doped fluorides have poor water resistance and poor fluorescence performance at high-temperature. The Mn4+ doped fluoride phosphors are easily hydrolyzed into mixed-valence manganese oxide and hydroxide in a humid environment, leading to discoloration and causing severe fluorescence degradation during applications. It is well known that the operating temperature of WLEDs often achieves 150 °C, and the poor fluorescence performance at high temperatures is reflected in the sharp decline of fluorescent intensity with the continuous increase of the operating temperature. This phenomenon, known as thermal quenching, seriously affects the color display index, lighting life, and other performance of WLEDs.

In order to improve the water resistance of the Mn4+ doped fluoride phosphors, researchers have proposed various strategies, including forming a protective layer on the phosphor surface or surface passivation [26–29]. For example, in 2015, Nguyen et al. reported a strategy using alkyl phosphate coated on the surface of K2SiF6:Mn4+ [26]. Li et al. successfully synthesized a phosphor with remarkable water resistance and fluorescent thermal ability by coating a K2SiF6 shell on the surface of K2SiF6:Mn4+ [27]. Fang's team first proposed the double-layer coating strategy by using hydrophobic oleic acid and undissolving SiO2 to enhance the water resistance of the phosphors [28]. Liu et al. obtained K2GeF6:Mn4+ with good water resistance through surface passivation to form a rare-Mn4+ layer on the surface of K2GeF6:Mn4+ [30]. Zhang's group prepared a single crystal form of K2SiF6:Mn4+ with good water resistance based on in situ formation of a rare-Mn4+ layer on its surfaces [31]. Ye et al. reported the treatment of hydrolyzed Mn-doped fluoride phosphors by adding restoration modifiers. The luminescence intensity of the treated K2TiF6:Mn4+ maintained 62.81% of the initial one even after immersion in water for 300 min [32]. In order to improve the fluorescence performance of phosphors at high-temperature, many researchers have proposed different synthesis and treatment strategies. According to the report of Huppertz et al. Li2SiF6:Mn4+ phosphor was synthesized at 5.5 GPa and 750 °C under high temperature and high pressure through a HF-free green synthesis route, which exhibited good high-temperature fluorescence performance with no significant luminescence decay at 125 °C [33]. Lee et al. synthesized K3SiF7:Mn4+ with the assistance of organic solvents, and the luminescence intensity of the prepared phosphor was still able to maintain 77% of the initial intensity at 190 °C [34].

Quantum dots (QDs) have been gradually applied in photoelectricity as a semiconductor luminescent material due to their high quantum yield, color purity, and tunability [35,36]. Common QDs often contain heavy metal elements, which seriously threaten the health of human beings and the environment during application. Carbon-based nanomaterials, including GQDs, CQDs, CNTs, and many other materials, have attracted extensive attention from researchers due to their advantages of low cost, environmental friendliness, excellent mechanical strength, and photoelectric properties. Poddar et al. systematically studied the effects of GQDs on the morphology and luminescence of β -NaYF4:Gd3+/Tb3+ phosphors synthesized by the hydrothermal method [37]. Li et al. added Cl-GQDs into K2SiF6:Mn4+ phosphor, and the results showed that the phosphor still did not exhibit luminescence attenuation at 240 °C [38].

However, none of the above studies on phosphor doping GQDs improved the moisture resistance of phosphor; as part of a systematic study, we prepared a series of K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+ phosphors coated with GE-GQDs (GE-GQDs: green emission graphene quantum dots) by using hydrothermal methods. The experimental results show that the water resistance, emission intensity, and luminescent thermal stability of the optimal coated sample have been significantly improved. We believe that our synthesis strategy may provide ideal phosphors for the practical application of high-power WLEDs.

The contents of this section are provided in the supplementary materials (including (a) the Experimental section, (b) EDS spectra of the obtained K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDs, (c) Properties of green emission graphene quantum dots (GE-GQDs), (d) Equations of the NTQ).

The results of ICP elemental analysis showed that the contents of K, Si, and Mn elements in the samples were 35.22%, 11.90%, and 1.49%, respectively. Therefore, the molar ratios of K:Si:Mn in the sample can be calculated as 1.9988, 0.9401, 0.0602, and the corresponding molecular formula is K2SiF6:0.060Mn4+, demonstrating that approximately 73.4% of the Mn ions in the raw material were successfully incorporated into the sample.

The following equations can describe the chemical reactions for preparing the samples.
(1) 4KMnO4 + 20HF+ 4 KF = 4K2MnF6 + 3O2+ 10H2O
(2) 4KMnO4+12HF = 4MnF2+5O2+6H2O + 4 KF
(3)
xK2MnF6 + (1-x)K2SiF6 = K2Si1-x F6:xMn4+

Equations (1) and (2) can be attributed to the self-redox chemical reaction of KMnO4 under acidic conditions. Equation (3) is the cation exchange equation of K2SiF6:Mn4+. The product of Equation (2), MnF2, is soluble and remains in the filtrate. Thus, the Mn4+ doped conversion is about 73.4% (x1 = 0.08), and a similar result has been reported by Lv et al. [39]. Fig. 1 (a) shows the XRD patterns of samples K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+ and K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDs (6 mg/mol) phosphors which the two samples both can be indexed to agree with the K2SiF6 (PDF 75–0694) standard card, and no other impurity diffraction peaks are found after the introduction of Mn4+. The result indicates that the hydrothermal method can easily prepare the pure phase phosphor; moreover, the Si4+ is perfectly substituted by the Mn4+ in the lattice [40]. Besides, the result of Fig. 1(a) indicates that doping of GE-GQDs does not change the lattice structure of K2SiF6. The absence of the characteristic GE-GQDs peaks in the spectra may be that the doping amount of GE-GQDs is too low to produce diffraction peaks.

As shown by the FT-IR spectrum of Fig. 1(b), GE-GQDs have a characteristic peak at 1655 cm−1 (C = C vibration) [41], and the sample (ii) has a similar characteristic FT-IR absorption peak to that of GE-GQDs. However, the C = C vibration peak of the sample (ii) is redshifted to 1528 cm−1, indicating that GE-GQDs is not only encapsulated on the surface of the sample (ii), but the conjugated π-bonds of GE-GQDs may also have coordination with the Mn4+ ions of the sample (ii). The absence of characteristic absorption peaks at 1500-1700 cm−1 in sample (i) indicates the absence of GE-QDs. The strong, broad peak at about 3454 cm−1 for the three FT-IR spectra in Fig. 1(b) can be attributed to C = C vibration of GQDs, and O–H vibration of H2O [38].

The SEM images and the corresponding EDS spectra of the samples are depicted in Fig. 2 . The sizes of both the samples (i-ii) are about 4 and 5–8 μm, respectively. The sample (i) is a lamellar crystal with a smooth surface; however, sample (ii) is a cubic crystal with a relatively complete structure, and its surface is covered with many irregular particles.

According to the EDS elemental quantitative analysis results (Fig. 2(b–d)), typical elemental compositions of the samples are K, Si, F, and Mn. However, sample (ii) also contains element C, while sample (i) does not. The EDS mapping image of K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDs are depicted in Fig. S1, and it can be clearly seen that element C is uniformly distributed on the surface of the materials. The spot scan data listed in Table S1 similarly found the presence of element C. These results indicate that GE-GQDs are successfully coated on the surface of K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+.

The TEM images of the initial sample and the coated sample are exhibited in Fig. 3 . Compared to the smooth surface of the initial sample, and a clear shell structure can be observed in the coated sample. The images indicate that the coating with GQDs can form a protective layer on the phosphor surface, which can effectively prevent the hydrolysis of Mn4+ ions and the failure of the phosphor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of Mn4+ and GE-GQDs concentrations on luminescent properties
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 describes the relationship between the Mn4+ and GE-GQDs doping concentrations and the luminescent properties of samples. Fig. 4(a) shows that all K2SiF6:x1Mn4+ samples have similar PL spectra with different intensities. Inset in Fig. 4(a) depicts that emission intensity is significantly influenced by concentrations (x1) of Mn4+(x1 = molar ratio of Mn4+/(Mn4++Si4+)). When x1 = 8%, the intensity has a maximum value, and the corresponding sample is the sample (i). Fig. 4(b) describes that a coating of GE-GQDs significantly enhances emission intensity. Furthermore, in the inset, we can see that the relationship between the GE-GQDs concentration (y) (y = mass (mg) of GE-GQDs/1 mol of K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+) and the emission intensity is approximately parabolic, with the highest point at y = 6 mg/mol and corresponding to the sample (ii).

Fig. 5 depicts the luminous performance of the samples (i-iii). Fig. 5(a and b) shows the PLE & PL spectra of the samples (i-iii), and the emission peaks of the two phosphors are located at 631 nm under the excitation spectrum at 451 nm. As seen from the figure, there is no significant shift or broadening of the phosphor's excitation and emission peaks before and after being coated with the GQDs. Due to the 3d electrons being easily affected by the coordination environment, the spin allowed transitions 4A2g→4T1g, and 4A2g→4T2g, located at 356 nm and 451 nm, exhibit broad absorption peaks.

The emission of 2Eg→4A2g transitions is a double forbidden transition of spin and parity, which an electron dipole can hardly emit. However, due to the coupling between electrons and phonons, the forbidden transition is partially released to manifest multiple sharp narrowband emissions between 600 and 650 nm [5,39]. The emission intensity of the coated sample (the sample (ii)) is 2.55 times that of the K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+(the sample (i)); Fig. 5(a and b) also shows the emission and excitation spectra of GE-GQDs, GE-GQDs exhibit an emission peak located at 524 nm under the excitation at 451 nm. However, this emission peak does not appear in the PL spectrum of sample K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDs in Fig. 5(d). Although the emission spectra of GQDs are not consistent with the excitation spectra of the phosphor, the phenomenon that the luminescence of the phosphor is still enhanced may be due to the enhanced Förster resonance energy transfer effect from GQDs to Mn4+ ions. The works of other researchers have also confirmed this phenomenon and conclusion [42–46]. Zhang et al. reported the enhanced luminescence intensity of Ln3+ (Eu3+) ions by CQDs and attributed it to the enhanced energy transfer effect of CQDs to Ln3+ ions [46].

Based on data of the PL spectra in Fig. 5(b), the color coordinates of the samples (i-ii) are calculated. The calculation results (Fig. 5(c)) are as follows: (i) (0.6907, 0.3097), (ii) (0.6941, 0.3059), in which the two points almost overlap, indicating that coating GE-GQDs does not significantly affect color purity.

Fig. 5(e) shows the fitted fluorescence attenuation curves of the two samples at room temperature. The fluorescence lifetime of the coated sample is 9.21 ms, which is slightly higher than that of the uncoated sample.
The quantum yield of the two samples was also tested, and the quantum yield of the coated sample was 80.96%, while that of the uncoated sample was only 68.29%.

The surface of GQDs is rich in functional groups, including hydroxyl groups, carboxylic acids, amines, and many other groups. Transition metal ions interact with the functional groups, especially –COOH, –OH, resulting in the formation of metal-GQDs complexes [47–51]. Therefore, after coating with GE-GQDs, under supporting the interaction between GE-GQDs and Mn4+, not only the absorption of the excitation light by the sample is enhanced, but also the nonradiative transition probability (transition from 2Eg to 4A2g) is reduced, and the radiative transition probability (transition from 2Eg to 4A2g) is increased. The mechanism of the fluorescence enhancement caused by the coating of GE-GQDs can be depicted in Fig. 5(f), which is supported by the results of Fig. 5 (a, b, e).

The [MnF6]2- contained in Mn4+ doped fluoride phosphors are completely hydrolyzed when the phosphors come into contact with water molecules, which is why the phosphors are disabled in a humid environment. Since invalidation of phosphors causes a significant reduction in the luminescent efficiency of WLEDs, excellent water resistance is one of the important properties which is necessary for practical applications.

To further confirm whether the GQDs doped strategy can protect phosphors from the humid environment, the samples (i-ii) were directly immersed in deionized water for a different time. From Fig. 6 (a), it can be seen that when the samples were immersed in water, the yellowish color of sample (i) changed rapidly within 10 min and ultimately changed to brown within 60 min under the natural light, while the sample (ii) showed only a slight discoloration on the surface after 360 min of immersion.

Curves of integrated emission intensity of the samples (i-ii) are depicted in Fig. 6(b), and both the two curves exhibit decay, but the decay rate and degree of curve (i) are much larger than that of the curve (ii). When the time reaches 330 min, both curves tend to flatten and become a straight line basically parallel to the abscissa. The integrated emission intensities of phosphors K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+ and K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDs decreased from 100% to 11.26% and 87.74% after immersion in deionized water for 360 min, respectively. Results of Fig. 6 prove that the water resistance of the coated sample is much higher than that of the un-coated sample, further proving that after GE-GQDs coating on the surface of K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+, its moisture resistance is significantly improved.

Studies have shown that the water resistance of Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphors can be enhanced by forming an organic or inorganic protective layer on the surface of the phosphors [26–29]. The above studies provide the basis for the formation of an insoluble protective layer on the phosphor surface by GE-GQDs. Therefore, it can be learned from the above experiments that GE-GQDs successfully formed an insoluble protective layer on the surface of K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+, which substantially improved the water resistance of the phosphor and made the phosphor more suitable for high humidity working environments compared with K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+.

The operating temperature of high-power WLEDs rises with the increase in driving current, making the temperature of the phosphor encapsulated on the chip far above room temperature. With the increase in temperature, the non-radiative transition of Mn-doped fluoride increases, and the luminescence intensity decreases sharply. This effect is called thermal quenching, which seriously affects the luminous efficiency and service life of WLEDs [13]. However, the phenomenon of abnormal temperature-dependent PL intensities known as the NTQ effect has been widely reported recently, in which the integral intensity of phosphors increases with the increasing temperature. It has been proposed that the NTQ effect is caused by coupling the electric dipole forbidden transition of 2Eg→4A2g with the odd parity lattice vibrations V 3, V 4, and V 6 [52].

Fig. 7 depicts the thermal performances of the samples (i-ii) under the different temperatures. From Fig. 7(a and b), it can be found that temperature significantly affects the luminescence intensities of the two samples. Fig. 7(c) shows the temperature dependence curves of integrated photoluminescent intensities in the range of 580–680 nm. Both curves are approximate parabolic curves with the highest points; the maximum values and corresponding temperatures are (i) 135% at 90 °C and (ii) 238% at 180 °C. It can be seen from the figure that both the samples show the NTQ phenomenon in which the integral fluorescence intensity rises first and then falls with the increase of temperature, and the coating of GE-GQDs greatly enhances this effect. Compared with the phosphors with the NTQ reported by previous researchers, sample (ii) has a higher NTQ effect [21,22].

In order to reveal the mechanism of GE-GQDS enhancing the NTQ effect, we studied the integral fluorescence intensities of Stokes and anti-Stokes peaks of the samples (i-ii) under variable temperature conditions, as shown in Fig. 7(d). According to the curves, it can be concluded that both Stokes and anti-Stokes peaks of K2SiF6:Mn4+@GE-GQDs show an increasing trend before 180 °C, while the growth rate of the anti-Stokes peak is more significant than that of Stokes peak. Both the two peaks begin to decline after the temperature exceeds 180 °C. The intensities of the Stokes and anti-Stokes peaks of K2SiF6:Mn4+ increases to 90 °C and gradually decreases after the temperature exceeds 90 °C. The reason for the elevated anti-Stokes peak of the sample (ii) can be attributed to the fact that as the temperature increases, the number of phonons occupied in the lattice of K2SiF6:Mn4+@GE-GQDs increases, and the electron-phonon interaction is enhanced to lead to the anti-Stokes peak sidebands corresponding to the phonon absorption process gradually increase [53,54]. Eq. S1 is the relationship between photoluminescence intensity and temperature derived from the first-principles mode, and we give the Stokes and anti-Stokes formulas Eqs. S2 and S3 correspond to phonon emission and absorption, respectively. Eq. S4 describes the phonon occupation number as a function of temperature and confirms that the phonon occupation number increases with increasing temperature. In contrast, the sideband of the Stokes peak decreases with the increasing temperature, but its spectral width increases, making both the Stokes and anti-Stokes peak intensities increase with increasing temperature, which is also consistent with the experimental data in Fig. 7(d). Therefore, the NTQ effect can be summarized as an increase in the phonon occupation number, auxiliary induces an enhancement in the emission of 2Eg→4A2g with parity and spins forbidden [55].

We also tested the fitted fluorescence lifetime decay curves at different temperatures in Fig. 7(e and f); the fluorescence lifetime of both the samples decreased with increasing temperature. At 150 °C (the chip operating temperature), the lifetime of K2SiF6:Mn4+@ GE-GQDs is 7.39 ms, while that of K2SiF6:Mn4+ is 6.49 ms. The change rate of temperature-dependent lifetime decay of K2SiF6:Mn4+ is higher than that of K2SiF6:Mn4+@GE-GQDs.

In addition, color fluctuation in response to temperature changes is an important parameter to consider in the practical applications of WLEDs. Usually, we use chromaticity shift ΔE to express the color stability [56], and the details are provided in the supplementary materials.

Fig. 7(g) describes the chromaticity shift (ΔE) of the samples (i-ii). It is clear that with the increase in temperature, ΔE values of the two samples increase non-linearly, while curve (ii) is lower than curve (i). Fig. 7(h) shows the CIE color coordinates of the two samples at different temperatures, and the color coordinates of the two samples are slightly shifted in the red area. The shift of the coated sample is smaller than that of the un-coated one, which the shift may be due to the slight broadening of the sharp red emission peak. Therefore, the results of Fig. 7(g and h) indicates that the color stability of K2SiF6:Mn4+@GE-GQDs is higher than that of K2SiF6:Mn4+. Fig. 7(i) is the TG. curve of K2SiF6:Mn4+@GE-GQDs. The apparent mass loss starts at about 560 °C, indicating that the sample has a very high structural thermal stability.

According to the above experimental results, the K2SiF6:Mn4+@GE-GQDs has more stable and better luminescence performance than those of K2SiF6:Mn4+ at the chip operating temperature, making it an ideal phosphor for high-power WLEDs.

The samples (i) and (ii) were mixed with the yellow phosphor YAG:Ce3+ in the same ratio and assembled into WLEDs by coating them with epoxy resin on the blue light emitting InGaN chips. Electroluminescence spectra at a 10 mA driven current are shown in Fig. 8 (a). The spectra include three colors of light which include blue light (about 450 nm), yellow light (about 550 nm), and red light (about 630 nm).

The chromaticity coordinates of the WLEDs are obtained from the spectral data in Fig. 8(a), and the results are shown in Fig. 8(b). According to the position of the two samples in the chromaticity diagram, the WLED composed of K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDs phosphor emits a warm white light, and the quality of the light emitted is better than that of the WLED consisting of K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+ phosphor. The CCT of the K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+ is 4317 K, and Ra is 89.4 under a driven current of 10 mA, while the K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDs (6 mg/mol) exhibits a higher Ra of 95.1 and a lower CCT of 3928 K.

Fig. 8(c) shows the CCT curves of the two WLEDs at different driven currents. Line (i) increases continuously with the increase of the driving current, while line (ii) is basically parallel to the abscissa. A linear fit of the two curves shows that with the increasing drive current, the CCT change rate of the WLED (K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+) is 3.09, while the rate of change of the WLED (K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDs) is only 0.85. The Ra curves of the two WLEDs at different driving currents are shown in Fig. 8(d). Curve (i) decreases sharply as the driving current increases, while curve (ii) is essentially parallel to the abscissa, which means that the Ra of WLED (K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDs (6 mg/mol)) tends to stay the same as the current increases in the test range. Generally speaking, the chip operating temperature increases with increasing chip driving current. Therefore, the results in Fig. 8(c and d) indicate that the WLED assembled with K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDs (6 mg/mol) is more adaptable to the higher operating temperature brought by the high-power blue chip over the range of test conditions.

In summary, we proposed a new strategy using green light-emissive GQDs to coat on the surface of K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+ via the H2O2-free hydrothermal method. The luminescent performance of the optimal coated sample K2SiF6:0.06Mn4+@GE-GQDs (6 mg/mol) is significantly improved compared to the un-coated sample. The integrated luminescence intensity of the coated sample is 2.55 times that of the un-coated sample, and its water resistance is also greatly improved. The integrated luminescence intensity is still 87.74% of the initial value after immersion in deionized water for 360 min. Due to the existence of the NTQ effect, the integrated PL intensity of the coated phosphor at 180 °C is 2.38 times the initial value at 25 °C. Under the driving current of 10 mA, the mixture of the sample (ii), YAG:Ce3+(yellow emitting phosphor), and epoxy resin was coated on the blue luminescent InGaN chip to assemble the WLED to obtain CCT = 3928K, and Ra = 95.1 warm white light. The results show that the phosphor has good color, CCT, and Ra stability under the operating temperature of WLEDs, and is suitable for blue-based warm white light and high-power WLEDs.

Lin Wang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Tianman Wang: Investigation. Daishu Deng: Investigation. Xue Zhong: Investigation. Yan Yu: Investigation. Jiawei Qiang: Investigation. Sen Liao: Conceptualization, Supervision. Yingheng Huang: Review & Editing, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21661006 and No. 21965004), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region, China (Grant No. 2019GXNSFDA245022, No. 2020GXNSFAA159036), the Scientific Research Foundation of Guangxi University (Grant No. XDZ140116), the innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (Grant No. YCSW2020015), the Students Experimental Skills and Innovation Ability Training Fund Project of Guangxi University (No. S202210593143).

ce:section.ce:para:
